input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane system comprising a mechanical crane and its controller, configured for laboratory operations. The twin is well‑suited for digital twinning because its mechanical geometry is defined in detail, a validated kinematic model exists, and the controller enables real‑time data exchange between the physical actuator and the digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides optimal control algorithms, real‑time monitoring of crane state, historical data logging for trend and anomaly detection, interactive visualization of the crane’s 3‑D geometry and motion, and continuous validation modules that compare simulated behavior against measured sensor data to identify faults or drift.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on CAD representations of the crane and a mathematical kinematic model; the twin is built using freely available and commonly used software platforms, though the specific tools and frameworks are not disclosed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The twin’s orchestration is implemented using containerized components deployed on virtual machines. An ActiveMQ message broker mediates data flow from the physical system to the twin, while sensor telemetry and simulation results are stored in an InfluxDB time‑series database. The architecture follows a loosely coupled, event‑driven pattern, with no commercial twin framework employed.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with the physical plant is achieved through a Raspberry‑Pi equipped with sensors that publish telemetry to the ActiveMQ broker; the twin also adopts an AutomationML model for structural representation. Microsoft Azure services (including Azure IoT Hub and related cloud tooling) are used for downstream processing, visualization, simulation, and prediction, thereby establishing dependencies on on‑premise hardware, the message broker, and the Azure cloud stack.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin exemplars described in the documents use a mix of behavioral, simulation, and predictive models.  The GreenhouseDT exemplar implements self‑adaptive behavior for both the physical greenhouse and its twin, using an InfluxDB time‑series database to store sensor measurements and an ActiveMQ message broker to stream data between components.  Its twin logic is packaged into virtual machine images that also contain prerecorded data, facilitating reproducible experiments.  The Air Quality Management exemplar relies on an AutomationML‑based digital twin model, which is executed in the Microsoft Azure cloud platform.  This model integrates real‑time sensor data from a Raspberry Pi deployment (e.g., temperature, humidity, CO₂, and particulate matter) with simulation and predictive modules that provide runtime visualization, indoor air‑quality forecasts, and virus‑spread prevention scenarios.  Both exemplars therefore feature a data‑centric architecture that couples sensor‑derived data streams to computational models that control, simulate, and predict the behavior of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The documents explicitly mention a Raspberry‑Pi platform used in the Air Quality Management exemplar, outfitted with a set of environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, CO₂, and particle monitors) for the physical system.  In the GreenhouseDT exemplar the sensors are not enumerated but prerecorded data is supplied within the virtual machine images, implying a similar sensor suite that measures temperature, humidity, and potentially soil moisture.  Detailed specifications such as spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, data rates, accuracy, or precision are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Crane Interface is implemented as a Python program that connects to the crane’s controllers and continuously streams the crane’s state (e.g. position, velocity, actuator currents, load weight, and alarm flags) to the Logging Application. The Logging Application sequentially writes these state records to a TimescaleDB instance, effectively persisting all telemetry in a time‑series database. The data are sent over the local network using the default TCP/IP sockets exposed by the Physical Crane Interface; the only transport protocol explicitly mentioned in the document is Python‑level network calls, with no specification of higher‑layer protocols. State updates are transmitted continuously at a frequency determined by the Logging Application’s configuration, with event triggers such as task‑completion or alarm conditions resulting in immediate log entries. No explicit data‑quality or preprocessing steps are documented, but the integration into TimescaleDB provides timestamped, structured storage from which downstream services (Dashboard, Continuous Validation) consume the data.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the Software domain (Control Application) and are synthesized by the Trajectory Generator Service, which emits optimal movement trajectories computed by a kinematic model. Trajectory data are passed as Python objects (e.g., dictionaries containing waypoints and control limits) to the Physical Crane Interface, which serializes them into low‑level actuator commands compatible with the crane controllers. Confirmation of command execution is achieved by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares live sensor readings (pulled by the Logging Application) with simulated execution results, computing distance metrics and notifying operators via the Dashboard when thresholds are breached. Authentication and conflict‑resolution mechanisms are not detailed in the document; however, the system design assumes that only authorized services communicate via the MQTT broker, and the Dashboard provides a unified feedback and control point for operator intervention.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication relies on an MQTT broker for signalling, enabling publish/subscribe messaging between the Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and the Dashboard. The system architecture is a local, edge‑centric network where services are Python processes communicating over MQTT, TCP/IP stacks, and direct database connections to TimescaleDB. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not specified; reliability is implied by the use of an MQTT broker, but redundancy or security protocols (e.g., TLS, authentication) are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin stack is deployed on‑premises (edge) alongside the physical crane hardware, utilizing local compute nodes to run Python services (Control Application, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) and a TimescaleDB instance. Data storage is provisioned on the same local infrastructure, with sufficient capacity to archive all time‑series telemetry generated by the Logging Application. Scalability is not explicitly addressed, suggesting a single‑node deployment; availability is supported by continuous logging and live validation feedback, but no redundancy or high‑availability mechanisms for services or database are described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development follows a model‑driven approach using AutomationML for the digital twin model. The source code and twin artefacts are maintained in GitHub repositories, enabling version control and change tracking. The system is containerized using Docker and deployed on virtual machines, leveraging ActiveMQ for messaging and InfluxDB for time‑series data storage. The twin evolves through iterative evaluation against recorded sensor data, with behavioural adaptation cycles applied to both the greenhouse plant and its twin. Testing and validation are performed through runtime simulation and prediction scenarios exposed via an Azure‑based pipeline, although formal QA processes are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Three main decision‑support applications are provided: real‑time visualization of greenhouse conditions, simulation of future states, and predictive analytics for virus‑spread prevention in indoor spaces. Analytics are performed using Azure Machine Learning services and Python scripts in the cloud, while results are visualised via web dashboards. Users interact with the twin through these dashboards, selecting scenarios and receiving predictive insights; confidence metrics are not explicitly reported.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
